proposal.why: The PushA face-pull slot is a ghost — last logged there 2026-02-24. You run face pulls live on PullA, PushB and PullB.
The plan asks 4 sets on both leg days; you have never hit the fourth. Cut the prescription to 3 real sets or commit to the 4th.
Same lift, two days, 15 lb and a set apart — on LegsA it sits behind leg press and extensions and gets the tired end of the session.
Six weekly sets of extension against five of curl, on top of leg press both days. Front of the leg is out-earning the back.
It lands at the tail of a long session and reads as clock-punching rather than core work.

Your two prescriptions are backwards. Push A calls the heavy 8-10 range but your chest has already eaten four sets of incline by the time it gets there — you pressed 150x10. Push B calls the lighter 10-12 and you pressed 157.5x10, heavier, on the day that is supposed to be lighter. Flip the labels so they match where you are actually fresh.

Your two prescriptions are backwards. Push A calls the heavy 8-10 range but your chest has already eaten four sets of incline by the time it gets there — you pressed 150x10. Push B calls the lighter 10-12 and you pressed 157.5x10, heavier, on the day that is supposed to be lighter. Flip the labels so they match where you are actually fresh.

REVERSING the earlier proposal — it was wrong on the facts. 'Last logged 2026-02-24' is the date of your last Push A, not the last time you chose to do face pulls: only one Push A has run since, a 34-minute 21:58 session where the last THREE exercises collapsed together. Your actual record here is 22 of 24 sets across 12 sessions — 92%, better than most of your leg work. And this is the only external-rotation and only posterior-shoulder work in a 24-set day carrying 16 anterior sets. Put it back.

Face pulls are the only loaded lift in your plan with repMin = repMax (15-15), so the progression rule fires EVERY session and the load can only climb. It went 17.5 to 40 lb, where a rope face pull is a trap-driven high row, not a cuff movement — then sat stuck at 40 for six sessions. It is already back on that path: 20.5 to 25.5 in eight days. A 12-15 range lets it progress by control and reps instead of by the pin.
Same fix, same reason — the fixed 15-15 range forces load on a corrective on every day it appears.

THIS is the slot the compliance argument actually pointed at: 3 of 15 sets across 5 sessions, 20% — the deadest slot in your program. You already do leg extensions on Legs A at over 100% compliance. You decided five times running that this duplicate is not worth doing; the prescription should say so instead of generating false 'stale slot' signals.

Every bit of core work you do is spinal flexion — 6 cable-crunch sets plus leg raises — against deadlifts topping 255x5 and rows at 150x8. Nothing resists rotation. Plank is also 3 of 10 sets across 5 sessions (30%), the second-deadest slot. NOTE: this swaps in another flexion movement because the catalog has no anti-rotation exercise yet — a Pallof press would be the right answer and needs adding to plan.json first. Decline this one if you would rather I add the right movement.

Your two chest-press prescriptions are backwards. Push A is labelled the heavy 8-10 range, but the machine sits behind four sets of incline there, so you pressed 150x10. Push B is labelled the lighter 10-12 and you pressed 157.5x10 — heavier, on the day meant to be lighter. Flip the labels so Push A is the 10-12 exposure and Push B the heavy 8-10. (Re-surfaced from yesterday; still unactioned.)
Same fix — make Push B the heavy 8-10 chest exposure, since that is where your chest is actually fresh and you press heavier.

The same lift is two different athletes across the split: 54x10 for three sets on Push B (fresh) versus only 47.5x13 for two sets on Push A, where it sits sixth after both presses, the crossover, the shoulder press and the lateral raise. Move it ahead of the Push A lateral raise so its one Push A exposure starts on fresh arms instead of the dregs.

Your legs were 100% bilateral — no single-leg work, so a stronger leg can hide a left/right gap that is judged on stage. A 4-seat trainer panel picked this over the Smith squat (bilateral, fixes nothing) and kettlebells (cap 30 lb, too light) as the one move that adds a squat pattern AND fixes symmetry. Start ~30/hand and light: the first sessions are to find balance and each leg's baseline. Weaker leg first, match — never beat — its reps. Off-ramp: if balance/knee/recovery suffers near prep, swap to single-leg leg press.
Keep quad volume about flat while the split squat is added: trim LegsA leg extension 3→2 sets (net +1 set on the day). Reverse anytime.
